text: The laparoscopic procedure involves creating a duodenal jejunostomy to address superior mesenteric artery syndrome, with visible manipulation of the duodenum and jejunum using laparoscopic instruments.

Sixty-two-year-old female presented with a complicated history, including anti-reflux surgery. She had poor oral intake and persistent weight loss after surgery. On her initial workup, she was diagnosed with delayed gastric emptying. Laparoscopic pyloroplasty resolved her symptoms partially.

Further workup showed normal gastric emptying, but an upper GI demonstrating bowel obstruction at junction of third and fourth portion of duodenum, attributed to superior mesenteric artery syndrome. Upper GI endoscopy confirmed extraluminal compression in this area.
She was taken to the operating room for laparoscopic duodenal jejunostomy after nutritional optimization with jejunal feeding.

The surgeon retracts the transverse colon and omentum cephalad, exposing the ligament of Treitz. The dilated third portion of the duodenum is visible to the right of the middle colic vessels. A window is created in the transverse mesocolon in this area.
The surgeon performs a generous Cocker maneuver, using blunt dissection to free the duodenum from its retroperitoneal and mesocolic attachments.

The surgeon draws the jejunum forty centimeters from the ligament of Treitz and positions the loop adjacent to the duodenum, ensuring there is no tension at the proximal site of the distal obstruction.

The surgeon places a stay suture and creates entotomies using hook electrocautery. A side-to-side anastomosis is formed with a 60mm laparoscopic linear stapling device, utilizing a 2.5mm staple load. The common entrotomy is closed with a running IIO absorbable suture in a single layer.

The surgeon places a reinforcement suture at the apex of the anastomosis to minimize strain, then sutures the efferent limb of the jejunum to the retroperitoneum using permanent sutures to prevent kinking.
The surgeon performs intraoperative endoscopy to visually assess the anastomosis site, confirming the absence of air leaks during the evaluation.

The patient did well following surgery. She was gradually advanced to a soft diet and was discharged home on post-operative day two. At her one month post-operative visit, she had gained two point three kilograms since surgery.
